title: The Size of Things 📏
description: Use the sizeof operator to inspect memory.

slides.title: Bytes and Memory 💾
slides.content:
A computer's memory is measured in **Bytes**.

Every time you create a variable, C reserves a specific number of bytes in RAM depending on the data type.

Want to know exactly how much space your variables take up? Meet the `sizeof()` operator.

slides.title: The sizeof Operator
slides.content:
`sizeof(type)` returns the amount of memory (in bytes) requested by that data type.

Notice we use `%lu` (long unsigned int) to print sizes, but modern compilers also accept `%zu`.

quizData.question: Based on most modern systems, how many bytes does a `char` variable occupy?
quizData.explanation: A char represents a single character and only requires 1 byte of memory.

slides.title: Why does Size Matter? 🧠
slides.content:
In high-level languages like Python, you never worry about variable sizes. The language wastes memory automatically scaling things up for you.

In C, you are placed in the driver's seat. Standard sizes on modern 64-bit systems are:
* `char`: 1 byte
* `int`: 4 bytes
* `float`: 4 bytes
* `double`: 8 bytes

If you are programming a smart watch with tiny memory, choosing the right size is critical!

quizData.question: Which data type usually requires the most memory?
quizData.explanation: A double usually takes 8 bytes of memory, which is twice the space of a standard int or float.
